title-group.article-title: Research progress on the interaction between forest carbon sequestration and climate change

abstract.p: Global climate change represents one of the most critical environmental challenges of the twenty-first century. As a key component of the terrestrial carbon cycle, forest ecosystems play a pivotal role in mitigating global warming. This paper provides a systematic review of the dynamic measurement methods for forest carbon sequestration, examines the interaction mechanisms between forest carbon sequestration and climate change, and studies the feedback mechanism of forest carbon sinks on climate change and regional differences. Results reveal that climate change influences forest carbon sequestration through various pathways, including rising temperatures, shifts in precipitation patterns, elevated atmospheric CO2 concentrations, and extreme climatic events. Simultaneously, forest carbon sequestration exerts a significant regulatory impact on the climate system. Future research should endeavour in the following areas: (1) Developing a consistent assessment system for forest carbon sequestration; (2) Quantifying multi-factor interactions; (3) Compound extreme climate events; (4) Make up for the observation shortcomings in key areas. This paper aims to provide references and guidelines for a systematic understanding of forest carbon sequestration and climate change interactions, as well as to promote scientific and technological developments in this field.

funding-source.institution: Key Technologies Research and Development Program（CN）

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has stated in its Sixth Assessment Report that emissions of CO2 and other greenhouse gases could lead to global warming exceeding 1.5 °C and 2 °C in the twenty-first century. From a physical science perspective, greenhouse gas emissions serve as the main drivers of global warming [67]. Climate change affects both natural ecosystems and socio-economic systems and is widely recognized as one of the top environmental problems threatening human survival [9]. Over the past decade, international climate governance mechanisms have diversified, with climate issues gradually being integrated into various policy domains. From the Kyoto Protocol to the Paris Agreement, climate cooperation has deepened, with research focusing on two pathways: emission reduction and carbon sequestration enhancement [62, 107].

Forests cover approximately one-third of Earth’s terrestrial surface and play a crucial role in carbon sequestration. Global forest ecosystems sequester approximately 120 ± 30 PgC per year, accounting for nearly 60% of terrestrial primary productivity [8]. As the largest carbon storage system in terrestrial ecosystems, forests absorb 33% of carbon emissions resulting from human activities [47]. Changes in forest carbon stocks directly affect atmospheric CO2 concentrations and the climate-regulating functions of forests [17, 85, 155].

Although extensive literature exists on forest carbon sequestration, the mechanisms of bidirectional interaction between forest carbon sequestration and climate change, particularly regarding differences across regional and temporal scales, remain insufficiently understood. This paper aims to systematically review the dynamic methodologies for measuring forest carbon sequestration, delve into their reciprocal interaction mechanisms with climate change and the associated regional variations, and explore future directions and policy implications. The content is structured as follows: (1) the basic definition and functions of forest carbon sequestration; (2) assessment methods; (3) driving mechanisms of climate change; (4) feedback mechanisms; (5) regional differences; and (6) conclusions and prospects.

sec.title: Basic definition and functions of forest carbon sequestration
Conceptual definition
sec.p: Forest carbon sequestration, as a primary component of terrestrial carbon sequestration, plays a significant role in reducing atmospheric CO2 concentrations and mitigating climate change [42, 106]. Based on carbon budgets, forests can be classified as either carbon sources (where emissions exceed sequestration) or carbon sinks (where sequestration exceeds emissions). Specifically, forest carbon sequestration refers to the process by which plants absorb atmospheric CO2 through photosynthesis and store it as organic carbon in plant biomass and soil organic matter [17].

The sequestration capacity of forests depends on net ecosystem productivity (NEP), which is the balance between carbon fixed through photosynthesis and carbon released via system respiration. NEP is a widely used indicator to quantify the magnitude of carbon sinks or sources [17, 27, 39, 40].

sec.title: Functions and processes
sec.p: Forest carbon sequestration encompasses several key aspects: (1) Carbon Absorption. Forest vegetation fixates carbon through photosynthesis, serving as the primary mechanism for ecosystem carbon storage. Mature forest stands can sequester an average of 2.4 ± 0.8 metric tons of carbon per hectare annually, with sequestration capacity depending on forest types, age structures, and climate conditions [84]. (2) Carbon Storage and Cycling. Forest ecosystems store vast amounts of carbon over the long term. The global forest carbon stock is approximately 861 ± 66 PgC, with about 44% stored in soil, 42% in biomass, and 14% in litter [96]. Forests cycle carbon between organic matter and atmospheric CO2 through photosynthesis and respiration. (3) Carbon Balance. This refers to the dynamic equilibrium of CO2 absorption and emission in forest ecosystems over time. (4) Climate Regulation. Forest carbon sequestration plays an irreplaceable role in mitigating global warming, serving as a critical natural pathway to counteract the greenhouse effect [62, 90, 158]. Forests also influence local climates through evapotranspiration, reducing surface temperatures and fostering microclimatic conditions that enhance carbon storage [37].

sec.title: Ecological and economic value
sec.p: Forest carbon sequestration generates multiple synergistic benefits, which are mainly ecological and economic. From the perspective of eco-efficiency, an increase in forest carbon sequestration may bring benefits such as climate regulation [97], oxygen release (Li et al., 2022), improved air quality [81] and increased biodiversity [63]. For example, Liu et al. [81] revealed the synergistic benefits of forestation on regional ozone air quality and forest carbon storage in South China, with an increase in forest carbon stock accompanied by an improvement in regional ozone air quality. Wu et al. [148] showed that in subtropical forests, the biodiversity of the herb layer exhibited a significant linear correlation with carbon storage: broadleaf forest carbon stocks increase with increasing species diversity. By enhancing forest carbon sequestration, multiple Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) can be achieved, including SDG13 (Climate Action), SDG15 (Life on Land), and SDG6 (Clean Water and Sanitation) [119].

From an economic perspective, forest carbon sequestration provides opportunities for generating international funding and market revenues through REDD + projects, while also improving local community livelihoods [35]. Cai et al. [16] highlighted that forest carbon sequestration functions as a natural resource with attributes of "resource, asset, capital, and funding." By clarifying forest property rights, forests can be transformed into capital for reinvestment and generate revenue in carbon trading markets. Zhang et al. [160] estimated that the maximum impact of forest carbon sinks on China’s GDP is approximately 0.25–0.26%, while developing countries can sell carbon reduction and sequestration credits to developed countries through Clean Development Mechanism (CDM) projects.

As the global carbon market evolves, forest carbon sequestration is increasingly recognized as a critical driver of low-carbon economic development [52]. The global forest carbon credit market grew from less than $100 million in 2016 to over $1 billion in 2021, with an annual growth rate of nearly 60% (Ecosystem [34]). This market-based mechanism provides economic incentives for forest conservation, although challenges such as permanence, additionality, and carbon leakage persist [143].

sec.title: Assessment and calculation methods of forest carbon sequestration
Overview of assessment methods
sec.p:
A variety of assessment methods have been developed to quantify forest carbon sequestration capacity. Currently, these methods are categorized into four main types: inventory-based data analysis, flux observation, "bottom-up" biosphere models, and "top-down" atmospheric inversion methods [155]. These approaches can be further classified into sample plot surveys, micrometeorological methods, model simulation methods, and atmospheric inversion-based estimation techniques. Each method differs in terms of spatiotemporal applicability, data requirements, and potential sources of uncertainty. Table 1 provides a comparative overview of the four main methods, highlighting their respective strengths, limitations, and application scenarios.Table 1
Comparison of major forest carbon sequestration assessment methods
Assessment methodApplicable scopeAdvantagesLimitationsSources of uncertaintyReferences

Micrometeorological methodsSmall to medium scales; ecosystem levelContinuous observation, high temporal resolutionExpensive equipment, sensitive to weather conditionsInstrument errors, data gaps, topographic restrictions, eddy covariance sampling errors sum to the order of 20—50 gC m−2 y−1[6, 78, 153]

Atmospheric inversion methodShort and long time series; regional to globalTop-down integration, independent validationLow spatial resolution, difficulty distinguishing sources/sinksSparse observation networks, regional biases, 48% bias in estimating regional forest carbon sinks by ignoring CO2 daily cycle[23, 91, 100]

sec.title: Sample plot survey method
sec.p: The sample plot survey method involves selecting representative areas, measuring parameters such as tree diameter at breast height (DBH), tree height, and crown width, and estimating biomass using allometric equations. Carbon storage is then calculated by applying species-specific carbon coefficients, with statistical methods used to extrapolate the results to entire regions. This method is particularly suitable for large-scale assessments across various forest types. The sample plot survey method has been applied in diverse ways across different studies. For example, Gibbs et al. [50] combined ground-based forest inventory data with remote sensing measurements and allometric equations to estimate carbon storage. Similarly, Fang et al. [39, 40] utilized forest inventory data and biomass expansion factors to assess changes in China's forest biomass and carbon storage from 1949 to 1998.

One of the key advantages of this method is its ability to directly measure forest parameters, ensuring high data reliability. However, it is both time- and labor-intensive, and its accuracy depends heavily on the representativeness of the selected plots. In recent years, integrated approaches combining high-resolution remote sensing data with ground-based surveys have become increasingly sophisticated, significantly enhancing both the efficiency and accuracy of forest carbon assessments [32].

sec.title: Micrometeorological methods
sec.p: The micrometeorological approach quantifies CO2 fluxes by monitoring wind direction, wind speed, temperature, and the vertical gradient of CO2 concentration within the turbulent boundary layer above a large, flat, and homogenous forest ecosystem [138, 151]. Common techniques include eddy covariance, gradient methods, advection methods, and pressure chamber methods, with eddy covariance being the most widely used approach. Eddy covariance, the core technique of micrometeorological methods, directly measures ecosystem-atmosphere carbon exchange by quantifying the covariance between CO2 concentration and three-dimensional wind velocity at high frequencies (10–20 Hz). The FLUXNET network, consisting of over 270 flux towers distributed worldwide, provides invaluable data for understanding carbon cycling across diverse ecosystems [6].

This method is highly suitable for studying carbon flux across various temporal and spatial scales, climatic zones, and vegetation types. One prominent advantage is its capability for continuous observation, offering detailed temporal resolution. However, precision still faces challenges such as underestimated nighttime flux, advancements in complex terrain, data loss, and scale expansion. [78, 138]. Key challenges include: (1) Systematic underestimation during nighttime stable conditions, when vertical mixing is minimal. (2) Horizontal advection effects in complex terrains, which can lead to errors in flux measurements. (3) Data gaps caused by sensor malfunctions or unfavorable weather conditions. (4) Scaling difficulties, as localized flux measurements may not fully represent larger-scale carbon dynamics. However, the continuous progress of technical means such as multi-source data fusion, error correction algorithms and remote sensing data assimilation is gradually improving their accuracy and applicability at different spatiotemporal scales [130, 135]. Moreover, the advance of various correction methods and gap-filling algorithms have been developed, significantly improving data reliability and usability [98].

sec.title: Model simulation methods
sec.p: Model simulation methods are used to simulate and quantify ecosystem carbon sinks in relation to environmental factors. Based on their parameters, structure, and algorithms, models are broadly classified into empirical models and ecological process models [78]. Ecological process models include biogeochemical models (e.g., BIOME-BGC, LPJ), terrestrial ecosystem models (e.g., TEM, CENTURY), and dynamic global vegetation models (e.g., LPJ-DGVM, ORCHIDEE), which are capable of capturing ecosystem responses to environmental changes. These models operate across spatial scales ranging from local sites to the global level and temporal scales from days to centuries, allowing simulations under diverse scenarios. For example, He et al. [58] applied the ensemble mean of carbon fluxes from three ecosystem models (CEVSA2, BEPS, and TEC) to simulate temporal and spatial variations in net ecosystem productivity in China from 1982 to 2010. Tagesson et al. [128] used LPJ-GUESS to simulate the carbon sinks of tropical and northern forests. Their results indicate that anthropogenic land use and land cover changes have increased the contribution of northern forests but decreased that of tropical forests to global terrestrial carbon sinks.

The primary advantage of model simulation methods lies in their ability to integrate multiple ecological processes, enabling long-term predictions and scenario analyses. However, these methods face limitations, including: (1) Parameterization difficulties, as accurately representing complex ecological processes requires extensive and precise parameter data [43]. (2) Validation challenges, due to limited availability of observational data for model calibration [77]. (3) Discrepancies between model outputs, which arise from differences in model structures, assumptions, and input data [66].

To overcome these challenges, advanced strategies such as model ensemble methods, data assimilation techniques, and model-data fusion approaches have been developed. By integrating outputs from multiple models with observational data, these methods reduce the systematic bias of individual models and enhance the reliability of predictions [65]. Furthermore, machine learning methods are increasingly being applied to carbon sequestration modeling. These methods effectively capture nonlinear relationships between variables and hold potential for improving model accuracy. However, they require large amounts of high-quality training data to ensure reliability and robustness.

sec.title: Atmospheric inversion-based estimation methods
sec.p: Atmospheric inversion methods represent "top-down" estimation approaches and include techniques such as Atmospheric Transport Models (ATM), Ensemble Kalman Filters (EnKF), These methods invert regional carbon exchange by assimilating information on carbon dioxide concentrations in the atmosphere, and are therefore also called carbon assimilation inversion models. Carbon assimilation inversion models can independently verify the results of other "bottom-up" carbon source and carbon sink estimation models [51, 78].

The core principle of atmospheric inversion methods is to infer the spatial distribution of surface CO2 fluxes based on observed changes in CO2 concentrations, combined with atmospheric transport models. To enable this, multiple global greenhouse gas observation networks and satellite platforms (e.g., OCO-2/3, GOSAT) have been established, providing abundant observational data for inversion studies [21]. For instance, Zhang et al. [161], employed the OCO-2 v10 MIP model to invert the carbon sink of Chinese terrestrial ecosystems. Their study found that from 2015 to 2019, the annual average of China's terrestrial carbon sink was about 0.54 ± 0.05 Pg.CThe primary advantage of atmospheric inversion methods lies in their "top-down" perspective, which provides an integrated approach to understanding carbon fluxes and serves as an independent validation tool for other estimation methods. However, these methods face several challenges, including: (1) Limited spatial resolution, as atmospheric inversion often struggles to accurately resolve fine-scale flux variations. (2) Difficulties in distinguishing specific carbon source/sink contributions, particularly in regions with mixed emissions and absorptions. (3) Regional biases, caused by sparse observation networks, especially in less monitored areas. To address these challenges, researchers have developed high-resolution regional inversion systems and multi-scale nested models that improve spatial and temporal resolution, thereby enhancing the reliability of atmospheric inversion outputs [117, 142].

sec.title: Integration of multiple methods and technological trends
sec.p: No single method can fully satisfy the demands of precise and comprehensive forest carbon sequestration monitoring. As a result, the integration of multiple methods has become a key development trend. Common approaches include combining remote sensing data with ground-based surveys or employing data assimilation techniques to incorporate flux observations into ecosystem models. Multi-scale integration methods are also widely used to cross-validate flux observations, remote sensing estimates, and atmospheric inversion results, thereby improving the reliability and accuracy of carbon sequestration assessments [116].

Technological advancements are reshaping the landscape of forest carbon assessment and monitoring. Key trends include: (1) Next-generation satellite sensors: These technologies enable more detailed monitoring of forest three-dimensional structures, such as canopy height, density, and biomass distribution, enhancing data accuracy and spatial resolution [121]. (2) Internet of Things (IoT): IoT systems improve the efficiency and precision of flux observations by providing real-time, automated data collection from distributed sensors, reducing the reliance on labor-intensive fieldwork [25]. (3) Artificial Intelligence (AI): AI algorithms, particularly in machine learning and deep learning, are revolutionizing data processing and model simulations. These methods enable the detection of complex nonlinear relationships, improve the efficiency of data integration, and enhance predictive accuracy [110].

By integrating diverse methodologies and leveraging emerging technologies, researchers and policymakers can achieve more precise, efficient, and reliable monitoring of forest carbon sequestration. These advancements not only support scientific research but also enhance the effectiveness of forest management strategies.

sec.title: Driving factors of climate change on forest carbon sequestration
Overview of climate driving factors
sec.p: Global environmental changes have significantly altered average climate conditions, including temperature, precipitation patterns, and the frequency of extreme weather events [68]. These shifts disrupt forest disturbance-recovery cycles, influencing carbon cycling mechanisms and the overall carbon sequestration capacity of forests [12, 44, 45]. Climate variables such as temperature, precipitation, humidity, and solar radiation are intricately linked to forest carbon sequestration, exhibiting sensitive interdependencies. Even minor changes in these factors can profoundly impact forest stability and productivity. Consequently, understanding how climate factors drive changes in forest carbon sinks has become a critical focus of contemporary research. The response of forest carbon sequestration to climate change is characterized by nonlinear dynamics and threshold effects. Moderate climate change may enhance carbon sequestration by extending the growing season, improving photosynthetic efficiency, and stimulating biomass accumulation. However, when climate changes surpass physiological thresholds, forests may experience increased tree mortality, reduced carbon storage, and, in extreme cases, a transition from carbon sinks to carbon sources [1].

These threshold effects are highly context-dependent, influenced by factors such as forest type, age structure, species composition, and local environmental conditions. For example, young and fast-growing forests may exhibit higher resilience to moderate climate variations, while mature or monoculture forests are often more vulnerable to extreme events. Understanding these complex interactions is essential for predicting and mitigating the impacts of climate change on forest ecosystems.

sec.title: The double-edged sword effect of temperature rise
sec.p: Temperature rise exerts a dual effect on forest carbon sequestration, acting as both a driver of growth and a source of stress. On one hand, global warming generally extends growing seasons and enhances forest productivity. On the other hand, when temperatures exceed ecological thresholds, stomatal closure, photosynthetic inhibition, and increased respiration rates may occur, accelerating the consumption of organic matter and inducing water stress [99].

Temperature directly affects the rate of carbon uptake (Fig. 1). Recent studies have highlighted the critical role of carbon uptake rates in carbon sequestration in terrestrial ecosystems, with increased carbon uptake rates contributing about 65% of the increase in total vegetation productivity in the Northern Hemisphere [82]. Within the appropriate temperature range, a warm climate promotes plant photosynthesis and accelerates CO2 uptake. However, above a certain threshold, high temperatures inhibit photosynthetic efficiency (Fig. 1). For example, in boreal forests of northern Europe, winter warming alleviates low-temperature constraints, promoting growth, but elevated summer temperatures may enhance respiration rates, reducing net carbon sequestration capacity [131]. The positive and negative effects of temperature on carbon sequestration also vary by region and ecosystem. In warmer and more arid regions, rising temperatures exacerbate heat and water stress, inhibiting vegetation growth and reducing carbon uptake and storage [31, 76].Fig. 1
The key components of forest carbon sinks and the impact of climate change on them Note: The values in the figure represent the major carbon fluxes on a global scale, with the unit being Pg C/yr. Among them, the data of photosynthesis (27.8 ± 9.5), plant respiration (27.4 ± 5.9), soil respiration (98 ± 13), and litter input (60 ± 20) were mainly based on Zhang et al. [159] and Pan et al. [97], Zhang et al. [159]; SRDB-V5 [73], Wu et al. [147–149]. Deforestation (approximately 1.34) was obtained based on Harris et al. [56] and unit conversion. Wildfire (2.2) data are based on van der Werf et al. [134]. The impact of pests on forest carbon sinks currently lacks a unified global quantitative estimation, and most are regional studies. Here, only a qualitative representation is provided. The contribution rate of the CO2 fertilization effect to carbon absorption is approximately 65% [82]. In the figure, the blue “(+)” indicates a positive effect on forest carbon sinks, while the red “(-)” indicates a negative effect. It should be noted that these flux values represent the carbon exchange rates of different processes, not simple balance of income and expenditure or the final net effect. The overall state of forest carbon balance should be evaluated through comprehensive indicators such as NEP, NEE, or NBP.

Rising temperatures affect carbon sink capacity by altering phenology. Studies indicate that the growing season has extended by approximately 2.6 days per decade in mid- and high-latitude regions of the Northern Hemisphere, enhancing carbon absorption. However, higher winter and spring temperatures can trigger earlier bud break, increasing the risk of late frost damage and partially offsetting carbon sequestration gains [102]. Additionally, warming accelerates soil microbial activity and the decomposition of organic matter, which speeds up soil carbon turnover (Fig. 1). In some regions, this process may result in net soil carbon loss, further complicating the carbon balance [26].

Climate warming causes the forest line to move towards higher altitudes and higher latitudes. As temperatures rise, alpine and the Arctic ecosystems are changing considerably, with tree lines moving upward and high-latitude boreal forests expanding, providing new space for vegetation cover. [57, 59]. For instance, the expansion of forests in the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, the Alps and the Arctic region may enhance the regional carbon absorption capacity [54]. In addition, due to climate warming, the successional trajectory of coniferous to broadleaf forests has been widely observed, which may increase carbon sequestration to some extent [83, 112, 113, 147]. However, the expansion of forests may also be accompanied by the release of soil organic carbon, especially as tundra thaws, and emissions of greenhouse gases, such as methane, may offset the gains in vegetation carbon sinks. [19].

However, rising temperatures could also offset carbon sequestration benefits by increasing the risk of wildfires and pests (Fig. 1). Global wildfire emissions contribute approximately 2.2 PgC annually [134]. Climate warming intensifies the frequency and severity of wildfires, disrupting the carbon balance of forest ecosystems. For example, the 2019–2020 Australian bushfires emitted an estimated 0.67 Pg of CO2, surpassing the country’s total annual greenhouse gas emissions by about 30% [13]. Climate warming also leads to an increase in the frequency and extent of pests and diseases and reduces forest productivity by damaging and even killing trees [61]. Evidence from some model simulations suggests that the long-term impacts of pests and diseases may lead to the reversal of forests from carbon sinks to carbon sources.For example, outbreaks of the North American mountain pine beetle (Dendroctonus ponderosae) and spruce budworm (Choristoneura burnijerana) often lead to carbon loss in North American forests. [33, 75].

sec.title: Changes in precipitation patterns and drought impacts
sec.p: Decreased precipitation and increased drought frequency lead to soil moisture deficits (Fig. 1). These deficits restrict tree growth and can trigger carbon starvation and hydraulic failure. Conversely, increased precipitation can alleviate water stress (Fig. 1). However, heightened cloud cover may reduce the availability of photosynthetically active radiation. This reduction partially offsets the positive effects of increased precipitation.. This phenomenon is particularly evident in Southern Europe’s Mediterranean region and parts of Northern Europe [131].

Global research predicts that drought events will increase in frequency, duration, and severity. Under warmer conditions, drought-induced tree mortality accelerates, further stressing forest ecosystems [89]. The impacts of drought vary considerable across regions. In tropical forests, prolonged drought and severely reduce carbon stocks and weaken sequestration functions [156]. In temperate forests, extreme drought events can temporarily reduce annual carbon absorption by up to 30%, with limited recovery capacity observed in subsequent years [22].

Changes in precipitation patterns also alter soil moisture availability. Even if total annual rainfall remains unchanged, shifts from stable, longer-lasting rainfall to shorter, more intense rainfall events amplify soil moisture variability. Furthermore, the shift from the past system of slow snow melting to one with less snow, faster snow melting, rain and snow events, and more rainfall will also have a substantial impact on soil moisture conditions. These processes will all lead to more unstable carbon fluthroughput in the ecosystem [74, 144].

The extent of drought impacts depends on the adaptive capacity of forest ecosystems. Physiological differences among tree species result in varied drought responses. For instance, under equivalent water potential conditions, conifers generally exhibit higher drought resistance than broadleaf species, while shallow-rooted species are more vulnerable compared to deep-rooted ones [15].

Biodiversity also plays a crucial role in alleviating the impacts of drought, as forest ecosystems with greater species diversity generally exhibit higher resistance and resilience. This is due to functional complementarity and the insurance effect. Diverse traits among species help stabilize ecosystem functions under stress [69]. These findings underscore the importance of conserving biodiversity as a natural buffer against the increasing risks posed by changing precipitation patterns and drought.

sec.title: The fertilization effect of elevated CO2 concentration
sec.p: The fertilization effect of elevated CO2 concentration is a critical factor in mitigating climate change, as it enhances photosynthetic efficiency and increases stomatal conductance (Fig. 1). Elevated CO2 promotes the coupling of global nitrogen and carbon cycles, increasing vegetation net primary production (NPP) and enhancing forest carbon sequestration [28]. Additionally, Walker et al. [137] observed that secondary forests exhibit significant biomass increases in response to elevated CO2 during early growth stages.

However, the extent of the CO2 fertilization effect is influenced by several key factors: (1) Nutrient Limitations: Nutrient availability moderates the potential benefits of elevated CO2. Terrer et al. [129] found that nutrient deficiencies can reduce the positive impact of elevated CO2 on plant biomass by approximately 50%. This limitation is particularly pronounced in ecosystems with low nitrogen or phosphorus availability, where the lack of essential nutrients constrains growth responses. (2) Water Stress: Under severe drought conditions, the positive effects of elevated CO2 on photosynthesis are significantly diminished. [55, 114]. Moreover, elevated CO2 may support forest recovery and improve stress tolerance following drought events. Nevertheless, it also poses risks. For instance, due to the increase in background biomass density, it may cause more carbon loss [157].

The long-term persistence of the CO2 fertilization effect remains a topic of debate. Some studies suggest that productivity enhancements persist for decades, although the magnitude diminishes over time [93]. Other research indicates that the fertilization effect may become saturated, particularly in nutrient-limited ecosystems, where the lack of essential resources constrains the long-term response to elevated CO2 [109].

Understanding the complex interactions between elevated CO2 and limiting factors is essential for accurately predicting the future role of forests in carbon sequestration. While elevated CO2 offers potential benefits for mitigating climate change, its effectiveness depends on the interplay of nutrient,and water availability. [109].

sec.title: Synergistic effects of extreme climate events
sec.p: Interactions between different extreme climate events exacerbate physiological stress on plants, often leading to nonlinear or synergistic effects on forest ecosystems. For example, coupled extreme precipitation and high-temperature events can produce complex outcomes (Fig. 1). Increased precipitation may expand the optimal temperature range for vegetation growth [53]. However, the combined effects of drought and heat waves intensify water stress, significantly reducing forest carbon sequestration, as observed in Mediterranean and southern temperate forests [131].

The frequency, intensity, duration, and timing of extreme events play critical roles in determining their impact on forests. Early-growing-season droughts typically have a greater effect on annual carbon uptake than late-season droughts due to their disruption of critical growth periods. Similarly, multiyear droughts can have cumulative effects that surpass forest recovery thresholds, triggering widespread mortality [1]. Extreme heat waves during critical growth stages are particularly detrimental, causing greater reductions in productivity compared to non-critical periods.

Compound extreme events, such as "hot droughts" (simultaneous drought and heat waves), have especially severe impacts on forest ecosystems. Studies reveal that combined heat waves and droughts exert far greater negative effects on forest productivity than either event alone, often resulting in large-scale forest die-offs [163]. For instance, the 2018 European drought and heat wave reduced monthly forest carbon sequestration by more than 50%, highlighting the vulnerability of forests to such extreme combinations [7].

Extreme climate events can also trigger cascading effects, further compounding their impact on forest ecosystems. For example, trees weakened by drought and heat are more susceptible to pest infestations, which exacerbate carbon losses and hinder recovery [120]. These cascading events not only reduce forest carbon sequestration capacity but also threaten ecosystem stability and biodiversity.

Understanding the synergistic and cascading effects of extreme climate events is crucial for predicting forest responses to climate change. This knowledge is essential for developing adaptive management strategies to mitigate the impacts of these increasingly frequent and severe events.

sec.title: Feedback mechanisms of forest carbon sequestration
Bidirectional interaction between forest carbon sequestration and climate change
sec.p: Forest carbon sequestration is deeply intertwined with climate change, operating through a bidirectional feedback mechanism. On one hand, climate change significantly influences the capacity of forests to capture and store carbon. On the other hand, the carbon sequestration process itself exerts feedback effects on the climate system. The balance between increased tree growth, stimulated by elevated atmospheric CO2 levels, and the stress or mortality induced by rising temperatures and intensified droughts determines the net carbon uptake of forests [36].

From the perspective of the global carbon cycle, this interaction constitutes a complex feedback loop. Forests play a crucial role in mitigating global warming by absorbing atmospheric CO2 and reducing greenhouse gas concentrations. However, climate change simultaneously alters the environmental conditions that regulate forest carbon dynamics, such as temperature, precipitation, and extreme weather events. These changes create feedback cycles that vary across temporal and spatial scales and can be classified as either: (1) Positive Feedback: Amplifying disturbances, such as increased tree mortality or reduced productivity, which weaken forests' carbon sink capacity and release stored carbon back into the atmosphere. For instance, prolonged droughts or heat waves can transform forests from carbon sinks into carbon sources. (2) Negative Feedback: Suppressing disturbances or enhancing growth, such as CO2 fertilization effects or forest regrowth following disturbances, which strengthen forests' capacity to sequester carbon and mitigate climate change impacts.

These feedback mechanisms are highly dynamic and context-dependent, influenced by factors such as forest type, age structure, biodiversity, and regional climate variability [60]. Understanding the bidirectional interaction between forest carbon sequestration and climate change is essential for predicting how forests will respond to future climate scenarios and for designing effective strategies to enhance their role in climate mitigation.

sec.title: Positive feedback mechanisms
sec.p: Climate change can trigger positive feedback loops, where increasing vegetation mortality and disturbance frequencies exacerbate carbon loss, forming a "warming-disturbance-carbon loss" cycle. For instance, drought accelerates vegetation mortality, releasing stored carbon into the atmosphere and further increasing CO2 concentrations. Simultaneously, high temperatures and water stress reduce forests' carbon sequestration capacity, weakening their role as climate buffers [152].

Positive feedback mechanisms exhibit obvious variation across ecosystems: (1) Permafrost Regions: Rising temperatures in permafrost regions cause thawing, which releases substantial amounts of long-buried organic carbon into the atmosphere. This process intensifies the greenhouse effect, amplifying global warming [118]. (2) Tropical Forests: In tropical forests, increasing drought and fire frequencies may trigger "tropical forest dieback," a phenomenon where carbon sinks are transformed into carbon sources. This transition significantly reduces the climate-regulating functions of these ecosystems [4]. (3) Boreal Forests: In boreal forests, warming temperatures promote insect outbreaks and increase the frequency of wildfires, leading to widespread forest mortality and substantial carbon losses. These disturbances further exacerbate the positive feedback loop [75]. (4) Changes in Forest Composition: Climate change can also alter forest community composition, introducing additional positive feedback mechanisms. Warming may favor heat- and drought-tolerant tree species, which often exhibit slower growth rates and lower carbon sequestration capacities [88]. For tropical forests, biodiversity plays a critical role in maintaining carbon storage and sequestration capacity. A decline in biodiversity may reduce forest resilience, further amplifying carbon losses [133].

Understanding these positive feedback mechanisms is essential for predicting the cascading effects of climate change on forest ecosystems. Mitigation efforts need to prioritize reducing disturbance frequencies, preserving biodiversity, and maintaining the integrity of vulnerable ecosystems to prevent further intensification of these feedback cycles.

sec.title: Negative feedback mechanisms
sec.p: In contrast to positive feedback loops, forest carbon sequestration also exhibits negative feedback mechanisms that counteract the effects of climate change. These mechanisms strengthen forests' ability to act as carbon sinks, mitigating further warming.

list-item.p: Enhanced Carbon Uptake Through Forest Restoration and CO2 Fertilization: Forest restoration efforts can rapidly enhance carbon sequestration. Elevated atmospheric CO2 concentrations promote photosynthesis and improve water-use efficiency, thereby strengthening forests' carbon sink capacity. This effect, however, is constrained by nutrient and water limitations. Global "greening" trends, characterized by increased vegetation cover, have partially offset carbon loss. The photosynthetic response to CO2 concentrations typically follows a hyperbolic relationship, and current atmospheric CO2 levels have not yet reached saturation, meaning forests retain the capacity to absorb additional CO2. Although physiological adaptation reduces the magnitude of this response over time, the direct effect of elevated CO2 on photosynthesis remains a dominant factor driving carbon uptake [132].

Expansion of Forest Distribution: Climate warming has expanded potential forest distribution in some regions, creating new opportunities for carbon sequestration. Boreal forests, for example, are expanding northward into formerly treeless tundra areas, while treelines are shifting upward in mountainous regions [57]. These shifts increase forested areas, providing additional carbon storage potential. Satellite observations between 1982 and 2009 indicate a significant increase in the global leaf area index, primarily driven by the CO2 fertilization effect [162]. This global "greening" has notably enhanced carbon sequestration capacity [103]. From 1982 to 2011, global gross primary production increased at an annual rate of 0.59 ± 0.12 PgC, partially offsetting anthropogenic emissions [20].

Ecosystem Adaptive Responses: Ecosystem adaptive responses to elevated CO2 may also form negative feedback loops. Over time, plants exposed to higher CO2 concentrations may enhance soil nutrient availability through increased fine root biomass and root exudates, which stimulate nutrient mineralization. This process alleviates nutrient limitations, sustaining carbon uptake [101]. Additionally, forests with high biodiversity exhibit species complementarity, where diverse traits among species enhance ecosystem function and stability. This complementarity strengthens forest resistance and resilience to climate change, maintaining stable carbon sink functions [69].

sec.p: Negative feedback mechanisms play a vital role in buffering the impacts of climate change by enhancing carbon sequestration and stabilizing ecosystem functions. However, the strength and persistence of these mechanisms depend on factors such as nutrient availability, biodiversity, and the extent of forest expansion. Understanding and supporting these processes is critical for leveraging forests as a natural climate solution.

sec.title: Scale dependency and temporal lag
sec.p: The feedback mechanisms of forest carbon sequestration exhibit scale dependency, with the strength and nature of carbon cycle-climate feedbacks varying across both temporal and spatial scales.

The influence of climate change on the carbon cycle differs across timescales: (1) Short Timescales (Seasonal to Interannual): On shorter timescales, direct effects of climate change on carbon fluxes, such as photosynthesis and respiration, dominate. Seasonal variations in temperature and precipitation drive rapid changes in carbon uptake and release. (2) Medium Timescales (Decades to Centuries): Over medium timescales, changes in ecosystem structure and function, such as shifts in species composition, forest age, and disturbance regimes, become more influential. These changes alter the long-term carbon storage capacity of forests. (3) Long Timescales (Millennia): On geological timescales, carbon cycle-climate feedbacks are governed by slower biogeochemical processes, such as soil carbon dynamics and the weathering of rocks, which regulate the long-term storage and release of carbon [60].

Feedback mechanisms also vary considerably across spatial scales: (1) Local Scale: At the local level, carbon cycle feedbacks are often driven by specific ecosystem processes, such as the CO2 fertilization effect or vegetation-induced biophysical changes (e.g., albedo and evapotranspiration). (2) Regional and Global Scales: Local processes may not scale linearly to regional or global levels. For example, while CO2 fertilization may enhance carbon uptake locally, these local enhancements do not necessarily manifest immediately or proportionally at larger scales. Temporal lags between vegetation response and climatic drivers mean that the benefits of CO2 fertilization or improved water-use efficiency may take decades to emerge, while large-scale biogeochemical feedbacks (e.g., changes in atmospheric CO2 concentration, nitrogen limitation) can overshadow local processes [11].Temporal lags in forest carbon sequestration feedbacks complicate predictions of ecosystem responses to climate change. These lags arise due to the delayed response of different ecosystem components to climatic changes: (1) Tree Mortality Lag: Tree mortality resulting from extreme weather events, such as droughts or heatwaves, often occurs years after the event itself. This delay reflects the cumulative physiological stress experienced by trees over time [2]. (2) Forest Structure and Composition Changes: Changes in forest structure and species composition, such as transitions to more drought-tolerant species, may take decades to fully manifest. These shifts influence the long-term carbon storage potential of forests. (3) Soil Carbon Pool Dynamics: Soil carbon pools exhibit the longest lags, often requiring centuries to respond to climate change. The slow turnover of organic matter in soils means that current observations may reflect the legacy effects of past climate conditions [123].

These temporal lags mean that the full impacts of current climate change on forest carbon sequestration may only become apparent decades or even centuries into the future. Similarly, observed changes in carbon dynamics today often reflect the legacy effects of past climatic events. This temporal complexity underscores the importance of long-term monitoring and modeling to better understand and predict forest-climate interactions.

sec.title: Strength and uncertainty of carbon cycle-climate feedbacks
sec.p: Quantifying the strength of carbon cycle-climate feedbacks is essential for predicting future changes in forest carbon sequestration and understanding their role in mitigating climate change. However, significant uncertainties remain due to several factors: (1) Regional Climate Variability: Divergent predictions of regional climate change, including variations in temperature, precipitation, and extreme weather events, contribute to uncertainty in estimating feedback strength. These regional differences complicate efforts to model global forest carbon dynamics reliably. Such as the significant uncertainties brought about by differences in climate scenarios: Under three different climate scenarios (SSP245, SSP585, SSP119), it was predicted that the variation range of carbon sink intensity (CSR) in China's terrestrial ecosystem from 2010 to 2060 was 0.270–0.542 Pg C yr−1, with an average of 0.423 Pg C yr−1. The peak times occurred between 2030 and 2040 (SSP245, SSP585) and 2040 and 2050 (SSP119) [154]. (2) Ecosystem Model Variability: Current ecosystem models often produce inconsistent simulations of feedback mechanisms, particularly regarding the CO2 fertilization effect, nutrient cycling, and the response of soil carbon pools to warming. The lack of consensus among models highlights gaps in understanding the complex interactions within forest ecosystems [46]. Based on the output results of the multimodal (TRENDY v11) simulation, the natural vegetation carbon sink from 2012 to 2021 was approximately 3.8 ± 0.8 PgC yr−1., and its uncertainty reflected the differences in CO2 fertilization effects among models [122]. (3) Knowledge Gaps in Key Ecological Processes: Limited understanding of critical ecological processes, such as the long-term impacts of biodiversity loss, species migration, and forest adaptation to climate extremes, further amplifies uncertainties. For example, the rates at which forests recover from disturbances or adapt to new climatic conditions remain poorly constrained. In the northern forests of northwestern North America from 1984 to 2014, although most of the AGB losses caused by fires and logging were offset by their recovery, the Earth system model still overestimated the accumulation rate of AGB. The increase predicted by the model was three times the actual observed value (+ 1,519 ± 171 Tg), and the model overestimated the carbon sink capacity of the northern ecosystem. It highlights the necessity of improving the simulation of fire and other interference processes [139, 140]. (4) Anthropogenic Land-Use Changes: Interactions between anthropogenic land-use changes (e.g., deforestation, reforestation, and land management practices) and climate feedbacks add complexity. These human-driven changes can either enhance or undermine forest carbon sequestration, depending on how they interact with natural feedback mechanisms [24].

As climate change intensifies, the strength and direction of carbon cycle-climate feedbacks may shift, particularly as global temperatures approach critical thresholds. Negative feedback mechanisms, such as CO2 fertilization and forest regrowth, may weaken over time due to resource limitations (e.g., nutrients and water) or physiological saturation. For instance, forests' capacity to absorb CO2 may reach a saturation point, beyond which additional CO2 has minimal impact on photosynthesis [125]. Certain processes may transition into positive feedbacks under extreme climate conditions. Examples include: (1) Permafrost Thawing: Rising temperatures accelerate the release of greenhouse gases (CO2 and methane) from thawing permafrost, amplifying the greenhouse effect. (2) Increased Wildfire Frequency: Higher temperatures and prolonged droughts lead to more frequent and intense wildfires, resulting in significant carbon losses and reduced forest cover. These shifts in feedback mechanisms heighten the uncertainty of future climate projections, as even small changes in feedback strength can have profound impacts on the global carbon cycle.

The strength and uncertainty of carbon cycle-climate feedbacks underscore the critical need for robust climate and ecosystem models, long-term observational data, and interdisciplinary research. As feedback mechanisms evolve and potentially amplify future climate change, urgent mitigation efforts and adaptive strategies are required to minimize risks and uncertainties in the Earth system's response to anthropogenic forcing.

sec.title: Regional differences in forest carbon sequestration
Overview of regional heterogeneity
sec.p: Forest carbon sequestration varies significantly across regions, reflecting the inherent spatial heterogeneity of forest ecosystems and their responses to climate change. Forests in different climatic zones exhibit distinct sensitivities to environmental changes. For example, boreal forests are particularly vulnerable to frequent wildfires and warming-induced pest outbreaks, while tropical forests are more susceptible to drought and biological disturbances, such as insect infestations and diseases [3]. These vulnerabilities highlight the importance of considering regional differences when evaluating global forest carbon dynamics.

Advances in machine learning and remote sensing have underscored the role of regional factors in shaping carbon sequestration patterns. Differences in vegetation physiological traits (e.g., photosynthetic capacity, water-use efficiency), soil types, and climate drivers (e.g., temperature, precipitation) influence the balance of the carbon cycle. Furthermore, spatial heterogeneity is amplified by variations in ecosystem responses to coupled carbon–nitrogen-phosphorus cycles, which regulate nutrient availability and forest productivity [127].

Several key factors contribute to this regional heterogeneity: (1) Climatic Conditions: Variations in temperature, precipitation, and seasonality strongly influence forest growth and carbon fluxes. (2) Soil Properties and Nutrient Availability: Differences in soil composition, nutrient content, and water retention capacity shape regional forest productivity. (3) Physiological Traits and Adaptive Strategies: The resilience and adaptive strategies of dominant tree species—such as drought tolerance or fire resistance—affect carbon sequestration potential. (4) Disturbance History and Forest Age Structure: Regions with frequent disturbances (e.g., wildfires, logging, storms) or younger forests often have lower carbon storage compared to mature, undisturbed forests. (5) Human Management Intensity: Anthropogenic activities, including deforestation, afforestation, and forest management practices, significantly alter regional carbon dynamics [10].

The spatial distribution of global forest carbon sequestration reveals marked regional patterns. Between 1990 and 2019, global forest carbon sequestration was estimated at approximately 3.56 ± 0.37 PgC annually, with tropical forests contributing the largest share (2.52 ± 0.41 PgC), followed by temperate forests (0.685 ± 0.05 PgC) and boreal forests (0.32 ± 0.04 PgC) [97]. This distribution reflects the high productivity of tropical forests, despite their vulnerability to disturbances, and the relatively lower but more stable contributions from temperate and boreal forests.

Understanding the factors driving regional heterogeneity is critical for improving the accuracy of global carbon cycle models and developing region-specific strategies to enhance forest carbon sequestration. Future research should prioritize integrating high-resolution climate, soil, and vegetation data to better capture the complexity of regional dynamics and their contribution to global carbon budgets.

sec.title: Responses of tropical forest carbon sequestration
sec.p: Tropical forests play a critical role in global carbon cycling, but their carbon sequestration capacity exhibits complex responses to long-term warming trends and anthropogenic pressures. While tropical forests have shown an overall increase in carbon sequestration capacity under elevated atmospheric CO2 levels, their sustainability is challenged by risks of carbon saturation in high-temperature and high-humidity environments. Moreover, deforestation and forest fragmentation driven by agricultural expansion remain key threats to tropical forest carbon stocks [79, 95]. The carbon sequestration capacity of tropical primary forests has further declined due to deforestation, secondary forest expansion, and recurrent droughts [97].

The response of tropical forests to climate change and anthropogenic disturbances varies considerably across regions: (1) Amazon Basin: Studies in the Amazon Basin reveal that while primary forests have maintained their carbon sink functions over the past three decades, their carbon absorption capacity has decreased by approximately 50%. This decline is primarily attributed to increased tree mortality driven by climate change, particularly higher temperatures and prolonged droughts [64]. (2) African Tropical Forests: In contrast, African tropical forests have demonstrated greater resilience to climate change, maintaining relatively stable carbon sink functions. This resilience is linked to milder climatic changes in the region and the stronger adaptive capacity of native tree species [64]. (3) Southeast Asia: Tropical forests in Southeast Asia have experienced severe impacts, largely due to high-intensity logging and extensive land-use changes. These activities have significantly reduced forest cover, degraded ecosystem integrity, and limited carbon sequestration capacity [108].

Tropical forest edge areas are particularly vulnerable to climate change and anthropogenic disturbances. Within 30–100 m of the forest edge, microclimatic conditions—such as temperature, humidity, and light availability—change significantly, leading to slower tree growth, increased mortality rates, and reduced carbon stocks [95]. As forest fragmentation intensifies, the impact of edge effects becomes more pronounced. In the Amazon region, edge effects alone have contributed to over 30% of total carbon losses, highlighting their substantial role in regional carbon dynamics [14].

The restoration and regeneration of tropical forests, particularly secondary forests, are critical for offsetting carbon losses caused by deforestation. Secondary forests and plantations have rapidly expanded, becoming important carbon sinks. Notably, tropical secondary forests absorb carbon at rates up to 11 times higher than primary forests, with peak absorption occurring during the first 20 years of recovery [105]. In tropical Latin America, secondary forests sequester an average of 1.46 ± 0.30 PgC annually, offsetting approximately 65% of carbon losses caused by deforestation [18]. This highlights the potential of secondary forests to mitigate carbon emissions and contribute to regional carbon budgets. However, the long-term sustainability of these carbon sinks depends on effective forest management practices and protection against recurrent disturbances.

The responses of tropical forests to climate change and human activities underscore their dual role as both vital carbon sinks and vulnerable ecosystems. Regional differences in resilience, the impacts of edge effects, and the growing importance of secondary forests highlight the need for targeted conservation strategies. To maximize the carbon sequestration potential of tropical forests, future efforts need to prioritize reducing deforestation, mitigating edge effects, and supporting the regeneration of degraded forests.

sec.title: Responses of temperate forest carbon sequestration
sec.p: Temperate forests exhibit dynamic responses to climate change, with their carbon sequestration capacity shaped by a combination of natural processes, historical land-use changes, and management practices. While the CO2 fertilization effect has temporarily enhanced carbon sequestration in temperate forests, this effect is increasingly constrained by drought and other environmental stressors. Additionally, the interaction between climate warming and edge effects has introduced further complexity. For example, warming can temporarily promote carbon sequestration in forest edges but simultaneously increases their vulnerability to climate extremes, such as heatwaves and drought [111].

One of the primary drivers of temperate forest carbon sequestration is historical forest recovery and large-scale afforestation efforts: (1) In Europe, widespread farmland abandonment during the late 19th and early twentieth centuries allowed for natural forest regeneration, creating significant carbon sinks [92]. This historical recovery has contributed to the region's long-term carbon storage. (2) In China, large-scale afforestation and ecological restoration projects implemented since the late 1990 s have substantially increased forest area and carbon sequestration capacity. [42].

The response of temperate forests to rising temperatures is highly nonlinear, varying by region and climatic context: (1) Cooler Regions: In cooler temperate zones, warming generally enhances tree growth and carbon absorption by extending the growing season and improving photosynthetic rates. (2) Warmer Regions: In contrast, in warmer temperate areas, rising temperatures often inhibit tree growth, particularly during periods of summer drought [5]. For instance, over the past 50 years, warming has enhanced forest productivity in northern Europe but significantly reduced it in southern Europe due to increased drought stress [131]. This nonlinear response highlights the importance of regional climatic conditions in shaping temperate forest carbon dynamics.

Temperate forests are increasingly threatened by pests and extreme climate events, which have caused substantial carbon stock losses in recent decades: (1) Pest Outbreaks: Bark beetle outbreaks in western U.S. forests, driven by warming and drought, led to significant carbon losses between 2000 and 2009 [49]. (2) Extreme Droughts: The European drought of 2018 caused widespread declines in forest carbon sequestration, with some regions experiencing over a 50% reduction in carbon absorption capacity [7]. These disturbances underscore the growing instability of temperate forest carbon sinks, despite their enhanced carbon sequestration in certain regions.

Forest management practices have a critical influence on temperate forest carbon sequestration capacity. Effective management strategies can enhance both the magnitude and stability of carbon storage: (1) Positive Practices: Extending rotation periods, reducing harvesting intensity, and promoting mixed-species forests have been shown to improve carbon sequestration by increasing forest resilience and maintaining soil carbon stocks [87]. (2) Negative Practices: Conversely, overly intensive forest management can reduce soil carbon storage by increasing disturbance frequencies and altering microclimatic conditions, thus undermining long-term forest carbon dynamics [71].

Temperate forests play an essential role in global carbon cycling, but their carbon sequestration capacity is increasingly influenced by a combination of climate-driven stressors, historical land-use changes, and management practices. While historical forest recovery and afforestation have significantly enhanced carbon storage, nonlinear responses to warming, pest outbreaks, and extreme climate events pose growing challenges to the stability of temperate forest carbon sinks. To ensure their continued role as effective carbon reservoirs, forest management needs to prioritize resilience-enhancing practices that mitigate the impacts of climate extremes and support long-term carbon storage.

sec.title: Responses of boreal forest carbon sequestration
sec.p: Boreal forest carbon sequestration responses to climate change exhibit significant spatiotemporal heterogeneity, influenced by regional climatic conditions, disturbance regimes, and species composition. While moderate warming can enhance tree species diversity and promote carbon sequestration, extreme climate conditions often exceed species adaptation thresholds, reducing forest stability and carbon sink functions. For instance, warming may stimulate vegetation growth in certain areas but simultaneously increase fire frequency, leading to substantial carbon losses [150]. Additionally, drought conditions, the high sensitivity of understory vegetation, and the reduced sequestration capacity of aging forests further contribute to declines in carbon storage potential [86].

The impacts of climate warming on boreal forests vary considerably by latitude: (1) Southern Boreal Forests: In the southernmost regions, warming exacerbates drought stress, inhibiting tree growth and reducing carbon absorption capacity. (2) Northern Boreal Forests: Conversely, in the northern edge of boreal forests, warming alleviates low-temperature constraints, facilitating tree growth and forest expansion [48]. For instance, treelines in Alaska have shifted northward due to warming, while treelines in central and eastern Canada have exhibited temperature-induced retreat, highlighting the localized and complex nature of boreal forest responses [30, 57].

The thawing of permafrost is an important manifestation of climate warming, which has a profound impact on the carbon cycle of northern forests. Permafrost regions globally store approximately twice the carbon content of the atmosphere. As permafrost thaws, there is a heightened risk of releasing previously stored carbon in the form of CO2 and methane (CH4), further amplifying the greenhouse effect [118]. In addition to permafrost thaw, increased wildfire frequency and intensity play a critical role in boreal forest carbon dynamics. Wildfires not only release a large amount of carbon emissions but also disrupt soil structure and vegetation recovery. For example, severe wildfires in Siberian boreal forests during 2019–2020 emitted approximately 244 MtCO2, illustrating the scale of carbon losses associated with such disturbances [145].

The adaptive capacity of boreal forests to climate change largely depends on tree species composition: (1) Mixed-Species vs. Monoculture Forests: Mixed-species forests generally exhibit greater resistance and resilience to climate stressors compared to monoculture conifer forests, which are more vulnerable to pests and extreme climate events [70]. (2) Species-Specific Carbon Sequestration Patterns: Significant differences in carbon sequestration capacity exist among tree species. Evergreen conifers and deciduous broadleaf trees display distinct carbon absorption patterns under varying climatic conditions, with broadleaf species often exhibiting faster recovery and adaptation to disturbances.

Boreal forests are highly susceptible to climate-driven disturbances, which have increasingly undermined their carbon sequestration capacity: (1) Fire Regimes: Climate warming has intensified fire regimes in boreal regions, resulting in more frequent and severe wildfires. These fires not only release large amounts of carbon but also disrupt forest regeneration and alter species composition; (2) Pest Outbreaks: Warmer temperatures have expanded the range and activity of forest pests, such as bark beetles, further contributing to carbon stock losses; (3) Soil Warming: Rising soil temperatures accelerate organic matter decomposition, reducing soil carbon storage and further amplifying feedback loops in the carbon cycle [97].

Boreal forests represent a critical component of the global carbon cycle, yet their responses to climate change are highly variable and increasingly constrained by warming, permafrost thaw, and disturbances. While moderate warming may enhance carbon sequestration in certain regions, the combined effects of drought, wildfires, and pest outbreaks threaten long-term carbon storage. The adaptive capacity of boreal forests, particularly in mixed-species stands, offers some resilience to climate stressors. However, effective management strategies and mitigation efforts are essential to stabilize the carbon balance and minimize future carbon losses in these vulnerable ecosystems.

sec.title: Key insights
sec.p: This review synthesizes recent advances in understanding the interplay between forest carbon sequestration and climate change, highlighting the critical role of forests in global carbon dynamics, the need for refined methodologies to address persistent uncertainties, and their complex responses to climatic drivers. The following key insights encapsulate the current state of knowledge:

list-item.p: Forests as Crucial Carbon Sinks: Forests are the cornerstone of terrestrial carbon sequestration, accounting for approximately 30% of global anthropogenic carbon dioxide absorption, playing a pivotal role in regulating the global carbon cycle and mitigating climate change. Their ecological and economic value highlights the necessity of prioritizing forest conservation and sustainable management.

Advancements in Assessment Methods: Evaluation methods for forest carbon sequestration have significantly diversified, ranging from ground-based measurements to remote sensing and machine learning models. However, uncertainties remain in complex terrains and data-scarce regions (such as tropical forests in Africa) where flux towers provide limited coverage. The integration of these approaches, coupled with emerging technologies, enables more precise and scalable monitoring.

Dual Impacts of Climate Change: Climate change affects forest carbon sequestration through multifaceted pathways, including temperature rise, altered precipitation patterns, elevated atmospheric CO₂ levels, and extreme weather events. These impacts exhibit a duality: moderate changes can enhance sequestration, while exceeding physiological or ecosystem thresholds may lead to carbon sink saturation or transitions to carbon sources.

Nonlinear Feedback Mechanisms: Forest carbon sequestration and climate change are interlinked through nonlinear feedback mechanisms. Negative feedbacks, such as CO₂ fertilization, can offset climate warming, while positive feedbacks, such as increased tree mortality, wildfires, and permafrost thaw, can exacerbate climate change. These interactions are highly dependent on spatiotemporal scales and ecosystem-specific factors.

Regional Variability in Sequestration Dynamics: Regional differences in forest carbon sequestration are pronounced. Tropical forests: Carbon sink capacity is declining due to deforestation, forest fragmentation, and drought, despite the rapid growth of secondary forests; Temperate forests: Sequestration is bolstered by historical afforestation, CO₂ fertilization, and forest recovery, but remains vulnerable to pests and extreme climate events; Boreal Forests: Carbon sink functions are increasingly constrained by warming, wildfires, permafrost thaw, and drought stress, with significant regional heterogeneity.

sec.title: Research gaps and challenges
sec.p: Despite significant progress made, there are still some knowledge gaps in understanding the interaction between climate change and forest carbon sequestration. (1) Due to inconsistencies in the definition of forest carbon sequestration in different studies or projects, i.e., differences in measurement standards, there is often a lack of comparability between estimates of carbon sequestration, and even for the same region there may be large differences in the results of the assessment of carbon sequestration. For example, due to conceptual differences in the quantification of CO2 emissions from anthropogenic land use and land use change (eLUC), estimates of eLUC may be biased by approximately 20%, and this discrepancy is likely to increase further in the future [126]. (2) The synergistic or trade-off effects of multiple climate drivers (e.g., temperature, precipitation, and carbon dioxide, etc.) on long-term changes in forest carbon stocks are still not adequately addressed, especially with respect to the non-linear response of different forest ecosystems. (3) Most of the current estimation methods are unable to accurately capture the impacts of extreme climatic events (e.g., sudden droughts, heat waves) on the forest carbon cycle, leading to uncertainty in the prediction of future carbon sequestration capacity. Such as, under the medium emissions scenario, the sign and magnitude of the potential for future above-ground carbon storage in forests predicted by different methods varies from an average total gain of 6.7 Pg C to a loss of 0.9 Pg C [146]. (4) Regional differences in data availability: tthe number of flux tower networks is small and unevenly distributed. Among the over 500 flux towers worldwide, less than 5% are located in tropical regions, and most are distributed in accessible secondary forests, resulting in the neglect of key ecological processes [115]. This makes it impossible to provide accurate estimates of global carbon sequelage and limits the representativeness of the data.

sec.title: Future research directions
sec.p:
To address these challenges, we propose the following research directions, specific problems and potential solutions:
Developing a consistent assessment system for forest carbon sequestration: Future research should focus on constructing a unified assessment system for carbon sequestration that applies to different scales and takes into account multiple methods, and solve the problems of poor comparability and low credibility caused by the current assessment results due to the differences in methodologies, data sources and scale effects, through the development of standardised protocols, integration of multi-source data and quantitative estimation of uncertainties. A unified system may be achieved through the combination of cross-scale observation and modeling: for instance, for large-scale (global, regional) research, multi-source remote sensing and Earth system models can be integrated to address long-term trends and regional differences. At mesoscale (different forest types and river basins), vorticity correlation is combined with dynamic vegetation models to capture feedback paths. The results of revealing the nonlinear response mechanism were achieved by using FACE/field control experiments and high-precision phenological cameras on a small scale (different regions and tree species).
Quantifying multi-factor interactions: Future research could delve deeper into the mechanistic study of forest vegetation on climate drivers by conducting correspondence experiments and adopting multi-model integration (e.g., free-air CO2 enrichment (FACE) experiments in conjunction with dynamic vegetation modelling), in order to sort out the non-linear impacts of factors on the carbon sink of forests, and the impacts of different factors on the different carbon allocations due to specific feedback processes in the vegetation. In addition, multifactor manipulation experiments (e.g., simultaneous manipulation of CO2, nitrogen deposition and moisture conditions) can be introduced in combination with ecosystem model sensitivity analyses [93, 124] for identifying the strength of interactions between different drivers with threshold effects,cross-model intercomparison experiments and Bayesian data assimilation methods can also be used to quantify forest carbon cycle's nonlinear response range and uncertainty to multifactor perturbations.
Compound extreme climate events: Future research can further explore the key processes and feedback paths of multiple extreme climate events (such as drought superimposed with heat waves, drought combined with pests and diseases) on forest carbon absorption, storage and turnover, and integrate and combine multi-scale field observation networks, control experiments and multiple models. It can also combine high-precision phenological data or data with higher temporal resolution (such as phenological cameras, SIF data) with process models to clearly identify the nonlinear superposition of forest carbon sinks caused by complex events and the resulting ecosystem-level chain reactions (such as changes in carbon retention time), and how these are feedback to the regional climate system.
Make up for the observation shortcomings in key areas: In the future, for key regions of the global carbon cycle such as tropical forests in Africa, a long-term and multi-element carbon flux observation network (such as eddy covariance flux towers) can be established and improved. In regions where flux towers are rarely distributed (tropical and frigid zones), future research can prioritize the development of high-density observation networks in these areas. At the same time, it is necessary to develop and optimize interpolation methods for the problem of missing flux observation data: Deep sets can be used as a method for missing CO2 flux time series interpolation to improve the uncertainty of missing data interpolation in CO2 balance. When the time data coverage is high, the data does not contain long-term missing data, and the data is not collected at high-latitude sites, the MDS method can be used for interpolation [135]. Furthermore, by integrating multi-platform remote sensing observations (such as lidar, microwave remote sensing, and SIF data) with ecological surveys, the spatial gap in regional carbon budget assessment can be filled, and the systematic uncertainty caused by model parameterization bias can be alleviated, thereby enhancing the accuracy and robustness of global carbon budget estimation.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study, "Research Progress on the Interaction Between Forest Carbon Sequestration and Climate Change", did not involve human participants, animals, or sensitive data requiring ethical clearance. Therefore, formal ethics approval was not applicable. As this study is based on data from published literature and secondary sources, no participation of human subjects was involved. Hence, consent to participate is not applicable.
